title: 10 best herbal teas for eye strain
intro_desc: Herbal teas for eye strain are a natural and soothing solution to alleviate dry, tired, and irritated eyes. These teas work by providing antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the eyes, thereby relieving symptoms of digital eye fatigue. Examples of herbal teas that help with eye strain include peppermint, chamomile, and calendula, which can be enjoyed hot or cold as a warm compress or used in an eye bath to provide instant relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold teas helps with eye strain because it contains antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe and calm the eyes. The tea's flavonoids, particularly lutein and zeaxanthin, are specifically beneficial for eye health as they absorb blue light and protect the retina from damage. Additionally, the tea's calming effects can reduce eye fatigue caused by prolonged screen time or reading, promoting relaxation and reducing redness and irritation.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Pot marigold tea contains antioxidants that help neutralize free radicals that can cause oxidative stress and inflammation in the eyes, reducing the risk of eye damage and discomfort.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce swelling and redness associated with eye strain, providing relief from dry, irritated, or inflamed eyes.

Antibacterial properties: Pot marigold tea has been shown to exhibit antibacterial activity against certain bacteria that can cause conjunctivitis, a common eye infection that can exacerbate eye strain.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, making them effective in reducing eye inflammation and promoting relaxation.
Flowers: Containing carotenoids and flavonoids, which help soothe and calm irritated eyes, reducing redness and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh calendula officinalis petals for at least 20 dried ounces to ensure adequate supply.
Rinse the harvested petals thoroughly with distilled water to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine one teaspoon of dried calendula flowers with 8 oz boiling water in a ceramic tea infuser.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, then strain and discard the solids while reserving the liquid.
Chill the tea by refrigerating it for at least an hour before serving, allowing its anti-inflammatory properties to take effect.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree teas helps with eye strain because it contains antioxidants and flavonoids that work to relax and soothe the eyes. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties help reduce redness and irritation, while its calming effects ease tension and fatigue in the eyes. Additionally, the tea's ability to improve blood flow and oxygenation to the eyes can help reduce puffiness and dark circles, leaving the eyes feeling refreshed and rejuvenated. Regular consumption of maidenhair tree teas can lead to long-term eye health benefits, reducing the risk of age-related vision problems.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: The tea's antioxidant compounds help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals, reducing the risk of age-related macular degeneration and other eye diseases.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds help reduce swelling and irritation in the eyes, alleviating symptoms of eye strain such as redness, itchiness, and dryness.

Vasodilatory effects: The tea's vasodilatory properties help improve blood flow to the eyes, which can increase oxygenation and nourishment to the tissues, reducing fatigue and discomfort associated with eye strain.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the maidenhair tree have anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in reducing redness and irritation associated with eye strain.
Barks: The barks of the maidenhair tree contain flavonoids that help to improve blood circulation and reduce fatigue, which can contribute to eye strain.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried ginkgo biloba leaves or 2-3 tablespoons of fresh leaves.
Combine measured ginkgo biloba with 8 ounces of boiling water in a tea infuser or heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the active compounds to dissolve and release their benefits.
Strain the tea into another cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Drink the tea immediately, ideally within 10 minutes of preparation, while still warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Kansas coneflower teas helps with eye strain because of its unique blend of antioxidants, anti-inflammatory properties, and soothing compounds. The tea's active ingredients, such as echinacea and flavonoids, help to reduce inflammation and relax the eyes, alleviating dryness and irritation associated with prolonged screen time or reading. Additionally, the tea's natural astringent properties help to tighten and calm the skin around the eyes, reducing puffiness and redness.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Antioxidants in coneflower tea, such as flavonoids and saponins, help protect eyes from damage caused by free radicals and oxidative stress, which can contribute to eye strain.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Coneflower tea's anti-inflammatory compounds may help reduce inflammation and swelling in the eyes, which can alleviate symptoms of eye strain, including dryness, itchiness, and redness.

Antibacterial properties: The antibacterial properties of coneflower tea may help combat bacterial infections that can cause eye irritation and infection, such as conjunctivitis or pink eye, which are common causes of eye strain.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins, the roots help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the eyes.
Seeds: Containing mucilage, the seeds soothe and calm irritated eyes, reducing redness and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh echinacea angustifolia flowers and leaves for drying or purchasing dried botanicals in bulk.
Measure one teaspoon of dried echinacea angustifolia mix into a teapot or infuser.
Heat filtered water to boiling point then let it cool for three to five minutes before pouring.
Steep the echinacea mixture in hot water for eight to ten minutes allowing the herbal properties to seep through.
Strain and serve immediately, using as is or adding honey and lemon to taste, while still warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender teas helps with eye strain because it has natural calming properties that soothe and relax the eyes. The herbal tea's potent antioxidants and flavonoids work together to reduce inflammation and alleviate dryness, which can cause irritation and discomfort. As the tea calms the eyes, it also promotes relaxation of the surrounding muscles, allowing for a sense of clarity and improved visual focus.
remedy_properties: Anxiolytic (Anti-Anxiety) Properties: Lavender tea's anxiolytic effects can help reduce stress and anxiety, which are common causes of eye strain, by promoting relaxation and reducing muscle tension.

Cytokine Modulation: Lavender tea has been shown to modulate the body's natural inflammatory response by reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines, which may contribute to eye strain and discomfort by reducing inflammation in the eyes and surrounding tissues.

Antioxidant Properties: English lavender tea contains antioxidants that help neutralize free radicals and oxidative stress, which can damage retinal cells and contribute to age-related macular degeneration, a common cause of vision loss.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help to soothe and calm irritated eyes.
Flowers: Lavender flowers contain apigenin, a flavonoid that binds to GABA receptors in the brain, promoting relaxation and reducing eye strain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 tablespoons of dried lavandula angustifolia flowers and store them in an airtight container for future use.
Bring 8 ounces of boiling water to a simmer and steep the lavandula angustifolia flowers for 5-7 minutes to release their essential oils.
Strain the tea leaves using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large cup, discarding the solids and preserving the clear liquid.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey as needed to sweeten the tea and improve its palatability for consumption.
Allow the tea to steep for an additional 2-3 minutes before serving, allowing the flavors to meld together harmoniously.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valerian teas helps with eye strain because it promotes relaxation and calms the nervous system. As we stare at screens for extended periods, our eyes can become fatigued and irritated, leading to dryness, redness, and blurred vision. The sedative properties of valerian tea help to reduce eye tension and alleviate discomfort. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory effects may soothe itchy or irritated eyes, allowing them to recover from the strain of prolonged screen use.
remedy_properties: Sedative effects: Valerian tea helps to calm the eyes and reduce strain by promoting relaxation and reducing stress, which can contribute to eye fatigue.

Antioxidant properties: Valerian tea contains antioxidants that help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to age-related macular degeneration and other eye problems.
Muscle-relaxing effects: Valerian tea helps to relax the muscles around the eyes, including the orbicularis oculi muscle, which can become tense and strained from prolonged screen time or reading.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of valerian are used to make teas that calm and soothe the eyes, reducing fatigue and tension.
Rhyzomes: Valerian rhizomes are valued for their ability to reduce inflammation in the eyes, making them an effective remedy for eye strain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality dried valerian root and measure out two teaspoons for each eight-ounce cup.
Combine the dried valerian root with one cup of boiling water in a medium-sized pot.
Steep the mixture for ten to fifteen minutes to allow the active ingredients to dissolve.
Strain the tea into a separate container using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Allow the tea to cool to room temperature before serving and enjoy within four hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's Wort teas helps with eye strain because of its unique ability to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the eyes. The herb contains flavonoids, which have been shown to improve blood flow to the eyes and relax the muscles around them. This can help alleviate dryness, itchiness, and blurry vision caused by prolonged screen time or other forms of digital eye strain. Additionally, St John's Wort teas may also help to soothe eye fatigue and reduce redness, promoting overall ocular health and comfort.
remedy_properties: Hyperforin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and irritation in the eyes caused by prolonged screen time or other environmental factors.

Hypotensive effects: The hypotensive compounds found in St. John's Wort tea can help lower blood pressure and reduce inflammation in the eye tissues, alleviating symptoms of eye strain such as dryness and fatigue.

Antioxidant activity: The antioxidants present in St. John's Wort tea can neutralize free radicals that can damage eye tissues and contribute to age-related macular degeneration and other eye disorders, helping to protect the eyes from oxidative stress.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in hypericin and hyperforin, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe and calm irritated eyes.
Flowers: Containing flavonoids and phenolic acids, they provide antioxidant protection to the eyes and may help reduce oxidative stress caused by eye strain.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried st john's wort flowers in adequate amounts for brewing a full cup.
Measure one teaspoon of the dried herb per eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for five to seven minutes in a heat-resistant container.
Strain and discard the solids before serving the tea warm or chilled.
Consume two cups daily, ideally during meals, for optimal eye strain relief.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with eye strain because it contains a wealth of antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that help to soothe and protect the eyes. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties reduce redness and irritation, while its antioxidant-rich compounds neutralize free radicals that can damage ocular tissues. Additionally, dandelion's natural diuretic effects can help to reduce puffiness and dark circles under the eyes, leaving them looking refreshed and revitalized. remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Dandelion tea contains antioxidants like vitamin A, C, and E, which help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals, reducing the risk of age-related macular degeneration and cataracts.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in dandelion tea have anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce eye inflammation and irritation, alleviating symptoms of dry eye syndrome and conjunctivitis.

Antioxidant-rich carotenoids: Dandelion tea contains carotenoids like lutein and zeaxanthin, which are deposited into the macula, helping to filter out blue light and reduce oxidative stress, protecting the eyes from damage caused by digital screen use and other environmental factors.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, dandelion roots help soothe and calm irritated eyes.
Buds: Containing flavonoids and other bioactive compounds, dandelion buds aid in reducing inflammation and promoting healthy vision.
remedy_recipe: Rinse 2 tablespoons of dried taraxacum officinale leaves in boiling water for 30 seconds.
Add 4 cups of fresh cold water to the rinsed leaves and steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large bowl or teapot.
Discard the solids and store the tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 2 days.
Brew 1-2 cups of the stored tea as needed, allowing it to steep for 5 minutes before serving hot or cold.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Chamomile teas helps with eye strain because its calming properties soothe and relax the eyes, reducing inflammation and redness. The antioxidants present in chamomile also help to protect the delicate tissues of the eyes from damage caused by prolonged screen time or other visual stressors. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory compounds help to reduce swelling and tension around the eyes, promoting a sense of relaxation and reducing eye fatigue. remedy_recipe: Measure 1 teaspoon of dried matricaria chamomilla flowers per 8 oz of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes in a teapot or infuser.
Strain the tea into a cup using a piece of cheesecloth or a fine-mesh strainer.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable temperature before consumption.
Drink the tea immediately after preparation, ideally 1-2 times per day.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eyebright teas helps with eye strain because it has natural anti-inflammatory properties that soothe and calm tired eyes. remedy_recipe: Harvest one tablespoon of dried or fresh achillea millefolium leaves and flowers.
Combine the herbs with two cups boiling water for seven minutes of steeping time.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove solids.
Add one teaspoon honey and stir until dissolved for a soothing flavor.
Drink the tea within 30 minutes of preparation while still warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Beach rose teas helps with eye strain because it contains antioxidants and flavonoids that reduce inflammation in the eyes, relieving tension and discomfort. remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh rosa rugosa petals and dry them thoroughly for at least three weeks.
Combine one teaspoon of dried rosa rugosa flowers with boiling water in a teapot.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes to allow the flavors and antioxidants to infuse.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemongrass teas helps with eye strain because of its ability to reduce inflammation and relax the muscles around the eyes. remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves of cymbopogon citratus, enough for the desired dose and steeping time.
Rinse the harvested leaves thoroughly with clean water to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried cymbopogon citratus leaves with 1 cup boiling water, allowing it to steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids and adding honey or sugar to taste if desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ironwort teas helps with eye strain because it contains antioxidants that reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the eyes. Barks: The barks contain flavonoids that help to strengthen blood vessels and reduce eye fatigue.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 teaspoons of dried sideritis scardica herb or 3-4 tablespoons of fresh leaves for every cup of water.
Boil 1 cup of pure water in a tea kettle or pot for 5-7 minutes.
Add the harvested sideritis scardica to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a clean teapot or cup, discarding any solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality verbena officinalis leaves and stems in the early morning.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dame's Jet Teas helps with eye strain because its unique blend of herbs works to calm and soothe tired eyes. The antioxidants and flavonoids present in the tea help to reduce inflammation and relax the ocular muscles, providing instant relief from dryness and fatigue. Additionally, the herbal ingredients promote healthy blood flow to the eyes, which can help to improve vision clarity and reduce eye strain caused by prolonged screen time or reading.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Dama Sage tea contains antioxidants that help protect the eyes from damage caused by free radicals, reducing the risk of age-related macular degeneration and other eye problems.
Anti-inflammatory compounds: The tea's anti-inflammatory properties can help reduce inflammation and redness in the eyes, which is often associated with eye strain and computer vision syndrome.

Vasodilatory effects: Dama Sage tea has been shown to dilate blood vessels, improving blood flow to the eyes and reducing pressure on the eyeballs, which can help alleviate fatigue and discomfort caused by prolonged screen use.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Rich in mucilage, which helps soothe and calm irritated eyes.
Barks: Contains compounds that reduce inflammation and ease discomfort associated with eye strain.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried hesperis matronalis flowers for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture in boiling water for five minutes and then strain it into a cup.
Add honey or lemon to taste if desired and stir well before serving.
Allow the tea to cool for a few minutes before drinking it slowly and thoroughly.
Drink the tea twice a day, once in the morning and once in the evening for best results.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat eye strain with herbal teas is by sipping on a calming brew of chamomile or peppermint. These herbs help reduce inflammation and ease tension in the eyes and surrounding muscles. Another option is to try a caffeine-free earl grey tea, which contains bergamot oil that can soothe and relax the eyes. Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried herbs in boiling water for 5-7 minutes, then strain and enjoy while gazing at a digital screen or taking a break from reading.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to eye strain that are treated with herbal teas are headaches, fatigue, and dry eyes. Herbs like chamomile, peppermint, and lemon balm are commonly used in teas to soothe irritated eyes and provide relief from tension headaches and migraines. Other herbs such as passionflower and valerian root help promote relaxation and reduce stress, which can contribute to eye strain and other related symptoms.
